item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this and I wonder if there's a way to "de-tale-ify" the Tale to make it 100% part of the article.
You're a really good writer!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now, I tend to be an emotional guy, but the level of emotion conveyed through this and accompanying documents was unbelievable. Poor Misty deserves so much better than to be stuck in a room with -D for the rest of her life. I hope they terminate the beast before too long.
+1, because this is a truly beautiful and terrible story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The concept of bizarre, anomalous families have always caught my interest, inside the SCP Foundation and out. This article was enjoyable: it had a setup that made you read on, and a vaguely frightening backstory to the whole thing. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is by far one of my favorite SCP's that aren't scary as emotionally impactful, I really felt bad for these guys. Man, upvote for me, and a definite upvote for the Tale attached, this is overall just really good, perfect blend of scary and sad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, I myself don't even consider the tale a standalone work anymore — it is linked to the article and essentially functions as a supplement. Yes, technically it was conceived as and still is a tale in and of itself, but at this point it is meant and set up to be read as part of the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Most people don't read tales. I draw the line at documents that are linked from the main page and intended to be part of the page, such as exploration logs. But tales, no.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: http://slatestarcodex.com/2014/08/11/does-the-glasgow-coma-scale-exist-do-comas/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What marching said. The main article is setup for the supplement; not reading them and thinking it lacks depth is like watching a movie trailer and thinking they didn't develop the characters properly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you think the narrative of the document is deficient, then you should read the author's supplement to this scip. c:

Anyways, ti's a good scip, so +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First, I'd like to point out that I like how the supplemental materials are structured in this one. Too many times, the article takes a break from the overall narrative to go into some detailed background document or a collapsible or what have you, and it feels like work to have to slog through the article, constantly going back and forth between different parts of the story and severely disrupting pacing. The way you've done this makes for a better reading experience. So that worked well.

The narrative content, however, has profound issues. For starters, there's this:

As a whole, SCP-2422 behaves and interacts similarly to an average middle-class suburban family, with typical alternating congenial and tense internal dynamics observed.

I get that this is supposed to be designed as some sort of avatar for the family experience as envisioned by American media (more on that later on), but there is absolutely no such thing as the "average middle-class suburban family" to the point that you can make scientific assessments about behavioral norms and dynamics. This may come across as somewhat of a nitpick, but it represents both the in-universe Foundation and the out-of-universe author having a fallacious view of what constitutes a family in real life, which misinforms and weakens the rest of the article. To put it more succinctly, you're kneecapping your sense of truth with this mindset, which keeps this from having any sort of thematic resonance.

This reliance on trope and stereotype continues with the focal point of your article, the teenaged daughter. Not only is she "depressed teenager" straight from central casting (complete with being molested for some reason), she is of course given empathy powers and emotion-sensing because of course teenage girl. I could have overlooked that, perhaps, in a different article, but again, the "average family" thing up front has already set the table.

"But they're supposed to be stereotypes" is the inevitable response, but the fact that the narrative interest is supposed to come from the fact that they in fact are not stereotypes and are not happy themselves is supposed to be some sort of surprise. That's only a surprise if you originally bought into the view that you espouse above about "average families." Couple that with yet another use of the Poor Girl in Containment archetype, its own obnoxious stereotype around here, and you've effectively robbed yourself of any ability to rise above cloying, shallow emotional reactions. It's the difference between finding something tragic, and finding it whiny and annoying.

The technical skill with which this was executed is evidently enough to ensure survival, but in my view, the narrative here is completely deficient.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's an issue that's come up across the site before, and one I'm very sympathetic with. Tim Scriven, who has professional experience in the area, has written a very solid post about it here: SCP's and IQ (Bonus discussion on personality tests as we.

I'm also a graduate student in psychology by trade. Nowadays, intelligence tests are made up of a bunch of subscales investigating various proposed aspects of intelligence, and theoreticians have to be very careful when talking about intelligence to define exactly what they mean and demonstrate that they aren't just measuring other factors. Modern test development is incredibly difficult (especially as we try to eliminate bias and clarify our theories). It's dicey, but it's important work, and we're trying.

If you wanted this to have a bit more modern scientific validity, I'd check out the description of the WAIS-IV here. It's one of the broadest used modern tests, and it would give you an idea of the components involved. You could say something like:

SCP-2422-A: When a human subject establishes direct eye contact with SCP-2422-A, it will gain understanding of the subject’s intelligence level, whether or not it has spoken or interacted with the subject at all. It rates the intelligence of subjects on a scale of 0-35, with the majority of tested subjects falling within 0.1 units of 22. These designations have been found to correlate significantly with most modern measures of intelligence.1

(Or whatever you want to call the Foundation test, if you want one. I just pulled that one out of my ass.)

If you wanted to get a bit more specific/weird with it, you could pick some of the smaller subscales of the WAIS-IV instead of the broader PIQ and VIQ summaries to imply that he's actually only measuring very specific things. That's probably getting out into the weeds, but you could PM me if you like.

Footnotes
1. e.g., Performance IQ and Verbal IQ ratings of the WAIS-IV, Fluid and Crystalized Intelligence Subscales of the Foundation Personnel Aptitude Test.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gosh, every other SCP I read about lately makes me feel so bad for it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I remember reading something similar to that somewhere, but it didn't cross my mind as I was writing this.

Is there a different test that's more up-to-date that I could use? The Foundation would need a point of reference to verify that SCP-2422-A's readings were accurate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The IQ test is outdated, inaccurate, and not really indicative of anything. They are are relic of Jim Crow era alienation and whenever people think of it as an "intelligence test" it just gets me mad. I know you did not know this, but "intelligence" testing has a very shady background. Look up something along the lines of "feeble minded" or "early 20th century intelligence test" and you will get what I am talking about. Although the modern IQ test is not really racist at all, it still does not mean anything. And before you jump to rebuke that, remember: correlation does not imply causation. Could you please maybe remove that bit referencing the IQ test? I don't think it will really subtract from the article in a meaningful way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hello!

Perhaps a classic lvl 4 clearance on the second interview could work, like "Irrelevant data has been removed from the following log and kept for the Project Director records"

Just an idea…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I kind of like the Wandering Father, but I really wish I had a better understanding of why the family has these particular anomalous properties, as they don't seem to follow from or lead into anything else in the article.

EDIT: Should have read the interviews. I'm gonna upvote this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, until I figure out a way to fit a link in the article, I'll put this here:

This skip is fleshed out much more in a new tale called Re: Promotion. It gives a lot more information about the current emotional state of SCP-2422, as well as their past involving Wandering Father.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you wish to define race, you can use "a person of European descent." More wordy, but communicates the idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah okay. I prefer boat though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …Stoat? Bloat? Quote?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for indulging my latest bugaboo. Upvoted!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, I did not know that. Thank you for pointing that out! I have removed the mention of race from the article, and will not use that word in the future.
Thank you again!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think I am starting to develop an aversion to "Caucasian".

+ show block
– hide block


It comes from racist 19th science (the original defined "Caucasians" as more attractive and moral than "Mongolians" ) and doesn't match current theories of human migration. It is still used in forensic anthropology, apparently, but this use is much broader than I think is intended by most authors here (it includes most populations in Europe, North Africa, Central Asia, and South Asia).


I also find the racial identity of the skip to be wholly irrelevant 99% of the time; here included and am novoting on this basis.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you so much for the upvote!

Don't worry about the rhyming. Maybe I'll write a story about a boat next.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice clinical tone
Although there wasn't a boat
This fact alone
Gets an up vote

(Obviously the upvote had nothing to do with a boat but I couldn't think of anything else that rhymed)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you. I've removed both passages. Thank you for pointing them out; reading them back over, I saw your point and the point Petrograd made above.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for your suggestions!

I've made changes based on all of them. I hope it works better now.

Thank you again!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A few things to address:

SCP-2422-B threatened to find his wife and tell her that he had sexual relations with Dr. ██████████ if he did not admit them immediately

Uh… that's a rather bizarre threat and it feels like oddly-placed humour here. I'm not sure if it has a place in the article, but the tone shift brought that excerpt was jarring.

I understand that it's -B's anomalous effect, but you can say things like "SCP-2422-B revealed classified information regarding the Foundation and personal information regarding [Guard]…"

I might have expected that kind of shit from a D-Class but never one of our researchers. I am disgusted and ashamed. –Dr. Welsch

Ditto whatever Petrograd said about the passage above.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this, but am no-voting for now.

My current issues:
My current issues:

If any of them are observed communicating with any entity outside the Foundation, all of them are to be terminated, due to the fact that the entity the members of SCP-2422 refer to as “Wandering Father” may pose a security threat.

This seems pretty extreme. I understand after reading the article the risk posed because they were deliberately inserted into the Foundation, but that's still a pretty extreme reaction. Also, is the containment area out in the open? Is it under a Faraday cage or continuous jamming? I'd like to see some evidence of prevention before the Foundation jumps to execution orders. I mean, they don't want to be forced to kill SCP objects because of a flaw in their own procedure.

- The language in the containment and initial description involving "claims", "resemblances", and "similar appearances" is way overboard. I get that they're (potentially) not humans, but even for clinical tone, the number of conditional statements is far too high. I nearly stopped reading the article in the second paragraph of the description, and would probably have downvoted there.

<Log 2> "Dr. Welsch: I’m not– crap."

This seems like a break in clinical tone. I understand that it's supposed to be a natural slip-up, but it doesn't feel natural to me, so it looks kind of silly in text.

They're called SCP-2422. I know you prefer to designate your own numbers for the entities in your custody, but I'm afraid I must be rather insistent about this one.

Is it just because they're recommendation 22? And is there any particular reason the Foundation would go along with this, especially when they believe that these "people" are a security risk?

All personnel who come into contact with SCP-2422-C are to be reminded not to allow it access to any electronic device.

This kind of seems like either a revision to the containment procedures or common sense/standard practice. I know that a reminder is appropriate in the circumstances, but this also gets to the point about prevention planning.

I might have expected that kind of shit from a D-Class but never one of our researchers. I am disgusted and ashamed. –Dr. Welsch

Addendum 2422-2 works fine, and implies this without the note. I understand Welsch's response, but this is neither the kind of thing that would stick around in an official document nor something that is furthering your narrative beyond what is already there.

Edit: Also, the bit with -B threatening the guard. I understand that this was a good way to utilize her ability, but it's kind of melodramatic (Oh no, they're cheating!), and points again to the Foundation not really thinking things through here. I tend to think that if you walk up to a Site and show some level of awareness of the Foundation, you get apprehended, questioned, amnesticized, and released at the bare minimum. Edit edit: MrWrong beat me to it :)

So, overall! The early language almost made me give up entirely. I really like the (pseudo)human drama of the entities, but the Foundation response seems rather wooden (not a huge problem) and their containment procedures a bit… non-comprehensive. Even a sentence or two about communication jamming or denying electronic equipment would help there.

As I said, no vote for now. Edit edit edit: Changed to upvote :)
